frameworks.name: ASEAN Power Grid (APG)
frameworks.detail: Long-standing framework (proposed late 1990s) for a fully interconnected regional grid by 2045. An Enhanced ASEAN Power Grid MOU was signed in 2025 to push multilateral (not just bilateral) power trade, with 18+ cross-border interconnections identified in the ASEAN Interconnection Masterplan Study.
frameworks.source_name: East Asia Forum / World Economic Forum

frameworks.name: Pan-Asia Power Grid Initiative (PAGI)
frameworks.detail: Launched by the Asian Development Bank (announced at ADB's Annual Meeting in Samarkand, ~May 2026). Targets mobilizing USD 50 billion by 2035 for cross-border transmission across Asia-Pacific, including the ASEAN Power Grid, South Asian connectivity, and the Caspian Green Energy Corridor. USD 25 billion is earmarked directly from ADB's own balance sheet, targeting 22,000 circuit-km of new cross-border transmission lines.

frameworks.name: ADB Regional Connectivity Fund for Energy (RCF)
frameworks.detail: Multi-partner trust fund launched 7 April 2026 to finance project-preparation work for cross-border energy and transmission infrastructure in Southeast Asia — the first facility of its kind in the region. Initial ~USD 25 million from Australia, Canada, Germany, the UK and the EU.

frameworks.name: Asia Clean Energy Forum (ACEF) 2026
frameworks.detail: 21st edition, held 8-11 June 2026 in Manila (ADB HQ), co-organized with the Korea Energy Agency. Theme: "Beyond Transition: Building Secure, Resilient, Inclusive, and Intelligent Energy Systems." Main venue where PAGI was formally presented to the region.

frameworks.name: ASEAN Ministers on Energy Meeting (AMEM)
frameworks.detail: Annual ministerial meeting coordinating the ASEAN Plan of Action for Energy Cooperation. The Philippines holds the ASEAN chairmanship in 2026 (Malaysia held it in 2025), with Singapore following in 2027 — each chairmanship shapes that year's energy cooperation agenda.

frameworks.name: Singapore International Energy Week (SIEW) 2026
frameworks.detail: 19th edition, 26-30 October 2026, theme "Connecting Energy Systems, Powering Tomorrow." Hosts the 4th ASEAN Energy Regulators Forum (with Singapore's EMA) and the Future of the Grid (FOTG) track covering regional interconnectivity.

development_plan.name: Revised Power Development Plan 8 (PDP8R)
development_plan.detail: Approved 15 April 2025 (Decision 768/QD-TTg). Estimates USD 136.3 billion needed 2026-2030 to nearly double generation by 2030 (USD 118.2bn generation / USD 18.1bn transmission grid). 2026 alone targets 87 new-construction and 86 completed transmission projects.

laws.name: Revised Electricity Law (2025)
laws.detail: For the first time allows private/non-state ownership of transmission grid assets, via the amended Electricity Law and updated PPP Law — though EVNNPT retains the monopoly on 220kV+ grid for national-security reasons. Implementation guidelines and risk-allocation mechanisms are still being finalized.

grid_operator.name: EVNNPT (National Power Transmission Corporation)
grid_operator.ownership: Wholly-owned subsidiary of state utility EVN (Vietnam Electricity)
grid_operator.grid_state: ~28,600 km of transmission lines (10,053 km at 500kV, 18,559 km at 220kV), 176 substations, ~115,650 MVA transformer capacity (2024 figures). Over 90% of business processes digitalized; grid remains overloaded in high-renewables regions.
grid_operator.source_name: US Dept. of Commerce Country Commercial Guide

foreign_agreements.detail: AFD signed a €67m (~$75-76m) financing agreement with EVNNPT (May 2025, during Macron's state visit) for two new 500kV substations in Binh Duong and Dong Nai. AFD is separately facilitating direct technical cooperation between France's RTE and EVNNPT — expert exchange on technical standards, cybersecurity, and HSE practices. Framed under the JETP (Just Energy Transition Partnership).

foreign_agreements.partner: GEDI (Guangdong Electric Power Design Institute, China)
foreign_agreements.detail: EVNNPT leadership is working directly with GEDI on HVDC standards development and technology transfer, with GEDI experts part of the consulting working group defining HVDC standards for EVNNPT's 2031 target lines. Positioned as a training/technology partner, not a financier.

foreign_agreements.partner: USTDA (US Trade and Development Agency) + Black & Veatch
foreign_agreements.detail: USTDA grant funded the international feasibility study (by Black & Veatch and Vietnam's Institute of Energy) for the 500kV Bac Lieu-Thot Not transmission line, submitted as a gift from the US to Vietnam. Grid connection agreement for the associated 3,200 MW Bac Lieu LNG plant signed March 2026.

development_plan.name: National Energy Transition Roadmap (NETR) + 13th Malaysia Plan (RMK13, 2026-2030)
development_plan.detail: The Sarawak-Peninsular HVDC interconnection is positioned as a flagship RMK13 infrastructure project.

laws.name: Electricity Supply Act + Energy Commission Act
laws.detail: Regulatory oversight sits with the Energy Commission (Suruhanjaya Tenaga). Peninsular, Sabah, and Sarawak remain three separate regulatory/grid regimes rather than one national code.

grid_operator.name: TNB (Peninsular) / Sarawak Energy Berhad (Sarawak) / SESB (Sabah)
grid_operator.ownership: State-linked (TNB majority government-owned via Khazanah and EPF); Sarawak Energy owned by Sarawak state government
grid_operator.ministry: Ministry of Energy Transition and Water Transformation (PETRA)
grid_operator.grid_state: Peninsular National Grid: 22,858 MW connected capacity, >420 substations, ~21,000 circuit-km of 132/275/500kV lines. Existing 300MW HVDC interconnection to Thailand (Bukit Ketri-Chuping to Sadao) plus HVAC links.

foreign_agreements.partner: International consortium (unnamed, NDA stage) — Sarawak-Peninsular HVDC
foreign_agreements.detail: TNB structuring a 51% local / 49% international equity JV for the 700km Sampadi-Sedenak/Sedili subsea HVDC link (up to 2,000MW, ~70% earmarked for Singapore export). NDAs with shortlisted international partners in progress; final consortium and full feasibility study targeted for early 2026.

TNB signed a joint development agreement with Singapore's SP Group (Oct 2025) for a second Malaysia-Singapore interconnector — a new 500kV substation at Pasir Gudang (Johor) linked via 2km submarine cable to Pasir Ris, Singapore — expanding beyond the existing 1,100MW export capacity.

Malaysia Energy Consortium (TNB + Petronas) signed a joint development agreement with Vietnam's PVM and Singapore's Sembcorp (May 2025) for a 700km submarine cable from Vietnam to Kelantan, linking to a 782km domestic 'supergrid' through Terengganu/Pahang/Johor before reaching Singapore. Feasibility due 2027, commercial operation targeted 2034.

TNB publicly stated it is exploring HVDC technology partnerships with Chinese state-owned utilities, citing their scale experience (China holds the world's longest HVDC link, 1,100kV/3,300km/12GW). Framed as a technology/experience partnership for ASEAN Power Grid HVDC build-out.

A separate Sumatra (Indonesia)-Melaka (Malaysia) interconnection is under pre-feasibility study with USTDA support, part of Malaysia's broader push for cross-border links beyond Singapore and Thailand.

development_plan.name: Philippine Energy Plan (PEP) / Green Energy Auction (GEA) program
development_plan.detail: GEA-5 (launched June 2025) is the first auction round dedicated to fixed-bottom offshore wind, targeting 3,300MW installed 2028-2030.

laws.name: EPIRA — Electric Power Industry Reform Act (RA 9136, 2001)
laws.detail: Restructured the sector and privatized the transmission concession (won by the SGCC-Monte Oro-Calaca consortium in 2008-09 for $3.95bn, 50-year franchise). The Philippine Constitution caps foreign ownership of public utilities at 40%. DOE has proposed amending the 2001 law to ban foreign-owned or foreign-government-linked entities from transmission control following the NGCP security controversy.

grid_operator.name: NGCP (National Grid Corporation of the Philippines)
grid_operator.ownership: 60% Synergy Grid & Development Phils Inc. (Filipino — Henry Sy Jr., Robert Coyiuto Jr., with the sovereign wealth fund Maharlika Investment Corp. having acquired preferred shares in Jan 2025) / 40% State Grid Corporation of China (SGCC)
grid_operator.ministry: Department of Energy (DOE); transmission concession originally granted by TransCo (govt-owned)
grid_operator.grid_state: NGCP states it has invested ~PHP300bn (~$5.3bn) strengthening the transmission system since privatization. PHP90bn Batangas-Mindoro Interconnection approved by ERC in 2026; NGCP targeting energization of major projects through 2026.

foreign_agreements.detail: SGCC holds 40% of NGCP since the 2008-09 privatization concession — the maximum allowed under the constitutional foreign-ownership cap. Under active congressional/Senate scrutiny since 2025 over alleged security risks (Chinese nationals in chairman/technical leadership roles, involvement of Chinese IT infrastructure provider NARI Group Corporation in NGCP operations). DOE has proposed legislation to remove foreign-government-linked entities from transmission control; no franchise cancellation has occurred as of mid-2026.
foreign_agreements.source_name: South China Morning Post / GMA News

foreign_agreements.partner: Maharlika Investment Corporation (Philippine sovereign wealth fund)
foreign_agreements.detail: Maharlika subscribed to preferred shares in Synergy Grid & Development Philippines (SGP, NGCP's majority Filipino shareholder) in Jan 2025, gaining two board seats each at NGCP and SGP — explicitly framed by the Marcos administration as regaining state influence over the grid amid the China-ownership controversy.

development_plan.name: RUPTL 2025-2034 ("Beyond the Greenest RUPTL")
development_plan.detail: PLN's 10-year Electricity Supply Business Plan, issued 26 May 2025. Calls for ~USD 183bn total investment: 69.5GW new generation (42.6GW new/renewable, 10.3GW storage, 10.3GW gas). Includes an explicit Smart Grid strategy covering HVDC development, FACTS, and digital substations, coordinated through PLN's Smart Control Centre.

laws.name: Law No. 30/2009 (Electricity Sector) + GR 42/2012 (Cross-Border Electricity Trade)
laws.detail: PLN retains statutory first priority to supply electricity and a de facto transmission/distribution monopoly. Cross-border power export requires meeting local demand first and unsubsidized pricing (GR 42/2012). MEMR Regulation 11/2021 governs electricity-business licensing structure.

grid_operator.name: PLN (PT Perusahaan Listrik Negara)
grid_operator.ministry: Ministry of Energy and Mineral Resources (MEMR / ESDM)
grid_operator.grid_state: Fragmented archipelagic grid; Java-Bali grid currently oversupplied and coal-dominated (69.5% of Java generation is coal-fired) while outer islands hold large untapped renewables potential (up to 209GW) but weak/absent grid connections — the core rationale for HVDC-VSC inter-island research.

foreign_agreements.detail: PLN and SGCC signed an MOU in November 2023 for smart-grid collaboration. As of mid-2026, described by analysts as still at the MOU stage with no concrete follow-on projects — the sector is seen as commercially unattractive and structurally opaque for large-scale Chinese investment compared to EVs/critical minerals.
foreign_agreements.date: November 2023 (MOU stage, no concrete projects as of 2026)

foreign_agreements.detail: USTDA signed a grant agreement with PLN for a feasibility study on two cross-border power interconnections between Indonesia and Malaysia, part of the 18-interconnection ASEAN Interconnection Masterplan Study. Framed as advancing the US-ASEAN Comprehensive Strategic Partnership and the Indonesia JETP.

TenneT's 2GW HVDC project has assembly/fabrication facilities in Batam (Cakrawala and Nusantara buildings, inaugurated May 2026), expected to employ ~7,000 local workers during construction — supplying HVDC components rather than a domestic grid concession.

development_plan.name: Power Development Plan (PDP)
development_plan.detail: Thailand's national generation/transmission planning framework under EGAT and the Ministry of Energy; TPA (Third-Party Access) Code reform is progressively opening transmission access to independent power producers.

laws.name: Third-Party Access (TPA) Code
laws.detail: Regulatory reform progressively opening Thailand's transmission grid to independent producers and direct power purchase arrangements — Thailand's equivalent to Vietnam's DPPA and Malaysia's CRESS mechanisms.

grid_operator.name: EGAT (Electricity Generating Authority of Thailand)
grid_operator.grid_state: Existing cross-border HVDC interconnection to Malaysia (Sadao-Bukit Ketri, ~300MW) plus HVAC links; central node of the LTMS-PIP multilateral trading corridor.

foreign_agreements.detail: Began as a pilot in 2014, fully operationalized in 2022 — the region's clearest working example of multilateral (not just bilateral) cross-border power trade, enabling Lao hydropower to reach Singapore via Thai and Malaysian transmission corridors.

development_plan.name: EMA power-import diversification roadmap
development_plan.detail: Singapore Energy Market Authority (EMA) is pursuing multiple cross-border import interconnections (Malaysia, Laos hydropower via LTMS-PIP, and other proposed links) to diversify its power supply beyond domestic gas generation.

laws.name: Electricity Market Authority regulatory framework
laws.detail: EMA regulates market access for cross-border electricity imports under Singapore's power-import licensing regime.

grid_operator.name: SP Group (Singapore Power)
grid_operator.ministry: Ministry of Trade and Industry / Energy Market Authority
grid_operator.grid_state: Domestic grid is mature and gas-dominated; growth is concentrated in new cross-border import interconnectors rather than domestic transmission build-out.

foreign_agreements.detail: Joint development agreement (Oct 2025) with TNB for a second Malaysia-Singapore interconnector (Pasir Gudang-Pasir Ris, 500kV/submarine cable) expanding import capacity beyond the existing 1,100MW link.
Singapore is the offtake end of the operational LTMS-PIP corridor, importing Lao hydropower transmitted through Thai and Malaysian grids.
